**Title:** Enhancing Adversarial Defense in Neural Networks by Combining Feature Masking and Gradient Manipulation on the MNIST Dataset

**Research Objective:** To evaluate the effectiveness of combining feature masking and gradient manipulation to enhance adversarial defense in neural networks, specifically for image recognition tasks using the MNIST dataset.

**Background:** Neural networks are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, which manipulate the input data to deceive the model. Feature masking and gradient manipulation are two techniques that can be used to enhance adversarial defense.

**Methodology:** The researchers used a conventional neural network architecture and integrated feature masking at various levels. They also explored different gradient manipulation techniques, such as noise addition, modification, gradient clipping, and smoothing. The models were evaluated on the MNIST dataset using the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) adversarial attacks.

**Results:** The baseline model without feature masking showed high accuracy (98%) but was vulnerable to adversarial attacks, with accuracy dropping to 60% under FGSM onslaughts. Models with feature masking exhibited a reciprocal relationship between masking proportion and accuracy, while also enhancing adversarial resilience. The results demonstrated the effectiveness of feature masking in augmenting adversarial defense.

**Contribution:** The study contributes to the development of more secure and dependable neural network frameworks by introducing a novel approach that combines feature masking and gradient manipulation. The results highlight the importance of diversifying features to prevent overreliance on specific input features and render the learning process less predictable and more resistant to adversarial attacks.

**Keywords:** Feature masking, neural networks, gradient manipulation, adversarial resilience, fast gradient sign method.
